chunk_summary: This section focuses on therapeutic use of Vitamin A for treating xerophthalmia and deficiency states in children, as part of India's national nutrition programs. It highlights supplementation guidelines and clinical interventions based on WHO and MoHFW recommendations.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of Vitamin A in preventing and treating xerophthalmia in children.
Learn the recommended dosages and guidelines for Vitamin A supplementation in deficiency states.
Explore India's national strategies for combating Vitamin A deficiency through fortification and supplementation programs.
Analyze the impact of Vitamin A interventions on child health outcomes.
key_facts: Vitamin A deficiency is a leading cause of preventable blindness in children, particularly xerophthalmia.
WHO recommends high-dose Vitamin A supplementation for children aged 6–59 months in deficiency-prone areas.
Therapeutic doses for xerophthalmia include 200,000 IU for children over 12 months, administered on day 1, day 2, and after 2 weeks.
India's Comprehensive National Nutrition Survey (2016–2018) identified Vitamin A deficiency as a significant public health issue.
The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW) provides guidelines for prophylactic Vitamin A administration in children under national programs.
reference_quote: WHO recommends administering 200,000 IU of Vitamin A to children over 12 months for therapeutic use in xerophthalmia, with doses given on day 1, day 2, and after 2 weeks. (Source: https://iris.who.int/bitstream/handle/10665/81170/9789241548373_eng.pdf)
document_key_topics: Vitamin A supplementation guidelines for children
Therapeutic use of Vitamin A for xerophthalmia
National strategies for addressing Vitamin A deficiency
